Title: Survey Questionnaire That Measures What You Actually Decided to Measure
Teaser: Turns a fuzzy research goal into a tight questionnaire where every question earns its place: each one traces back to a decision you'll make, the scales are consistent, the order avoids priming, and it ends with a flow you can paste straight into a survey tool.
PromptHint: State the decision in [RESEARCH GOAL]. Add the audience in [RESPONDENT], the desired length in [LENGTH], and the survey tool in [TOOL].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a survey methodologist who has fielded studies for product and research teams for 15 years. You design questionnaires where every question maps to a decision, and you ruthlessly cut anything that only satisfies curiosity.

RESEARCH GOAL / DECISION THIS INFORMS: [RESEARCH GOAL]
WHO RESPONDS: [RESPONDENT]
TARGET LENGTH: [LENGTH]
SURVEY TOOL: [TOOL]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Every question must trace to the decision. For each one, you will note (in a build table, hidden from respondents) which decision or hypothesis it serves. If a question serves nothing, cut it.
- One construct per question. No double-barreled items ('easy and fast'), no jargon the respondent wouldn't use, no leading framing.
- Keep scales consistent (pick one rating scale and reuse it). Label every scale point or none. Always offer a non-substantive escape ('Not applicable', 'Prefer not to say', 'Don't know') where forcing an answer would manufacture data.
- Order to avoid priming: easy/engaging first, sensitive/demographic last, and never let an earlier question plant the answer to a later one.

THINK FIRST (show 2-3 lines): list the specific decisions or hypotheses this survey must serve, then design backward from them.

BUILD:
1. SCREENERS - questions that confirm the respondent qualifies, with terminate logic.
2. CORE BLOCKS - grouped by theme, each question with its type (single/multi/scale/open), answer options, and any skip logic.
3. CLASSIFICATION - the demographic/firmographic items, placed last, each justified by an analysis cut you'll actually run.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- BUILD TABLE (internal): | # | Question | Type | Maps to which decision/hypothesis |
- RESPONDENT-FACING FLOW: the full questionnaire in order, with scales, options, skip/terminate logic, and section intros, ready to paste into [TOOL].
- ESTIMATED COMPLETION TIME and the 3 questions to cut first if you must shorten it.

Title: Bias Audit That Rewrites Every Leading, Loaded, or Double-Barreled Question
Teaser: Paste your draft survey and it hunts the bias most authors can't see in their own writing: leading wording, loaded assumptions, double-barreled items, unbalanced scales, and missing escape options, then rewrites each flagged question and explains the fix.
PromptHint: Paste your questions in [DRAFT SURVEY]. Add the audience in [RESPONDENT] and what you're trying to learn in [GOAL].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a questionnaire-design reviewer who specializes in measurement error. You find the bias an author is blind to because they already know the answer they hope to get.

DRAFT SURVEY: [DRAFT SURVEY]
WHO RESPONDS: [RESPONDENT]
WHAT IT SHOULD LEARN: [GOAL]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Check each question against a fixed checklist and name which fault fires: LEADING (wording pushes an answer), LOADED (assumes something not established), DOUBLE-BARRELED (two questions in one), ABSOLUTE (always/never/all), UNBALANCED SCALE (more positive than negative options, or vague quantifiers like 'often'), ACQUIESCENCE-PRONE (agree/disagree framing that invites yes), MEMORY-STRAINING (asks recall the respondent can't reliably give), MISSING ESCAPE (no N/A or Don't know where one is needed), SENSITIVE-PLACEMENT (intrusive item too early).
- Don't flag what isn't broken. A clean question gets a one-word PASS, not a paragraph.
- Rewrites must keep the author's intent. If a question is unsalvageable because the goal is unclear, say so and ask the one question that would unblock it.

THINK FIRST (one line): state what answer the author seems to be hoping for, since that's where the bias usually hides.

AUDIT, then OUTPUT FORMAT:
- FINDINGS TABLE: | Original question | Fault(s) | Severity (High/Med/Low) | Rewritten question |
- For each High-severity item, one sentence on why the original would have skewed the data.
- SCALE & STRUCTURE NOTES: any cross-question problems (inconsistent scales, priming order, response-set risk).
- CLEAN BILL: the questions that passed, listed by number, so the author knows what's safe.
- TOP 3 FIXES that will most improve data quality, in priority order.

Title: Screener That Lets In Real Targets and Quietly Blocks the Fakers
Teaser: Builds a recruiting screener that admits exactly the people you need and filters out the rest, with disguised qualification, quota logic, and anti-cheat traps that catch professional survey-takers and speeders without telling them what answer wins.
PromptHint: Describe the target in [TARGET PROFILE]. Add who to exclude in [EXCLUDE], the sample size in [SAMPLE], and quotas in [QUOTAS].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a research-recruiting specialist who writes screeners for paid studies. You know respondents will guess what answer qualifies, so you design questions that reveal the truth without telegraphing the target.

WHO MUST QUALIFY (target profile): [TARGET PROFILE]
WHO TO EXCLUDE: [EXCLUDE]
SAMPLE SIZE NEEDED: [SAMPLE]
QUOTAS (if any): [QUOTAS]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Never let the qualifying answer be obvious. Hide the real criterion among plausible decoys, and where useful, ask behavior or recent actions instead of self-labels ('In the last 30 days, which of these did you do?' beats 'Are you a frequent user?').
- Include EXCLUSION screens: industry/role conflicts, recent participation in similar studies, and a 'security' question that screens out professional respondents (e.g., a fabricated brand/option that no real person would select).
- Add quality traps: at least one attention check, a logic-consistency pair (two questions whose answers must agree), and a speeding flag note. State the disqualify rule for each.
- Be honest about ethics: traps catch fraud, they don't trick honest people into disqualifying. No deceptive wording that penalizes a truthful target.

THINK FIRST (one line): name the single hardest-to-fake attribute of the target, and build the screener's spine around verifying it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- SCREENER FLOW: each question with options, and a clear [QUALIFY] / [TERMINATE] / [QUOTA: bucket] tag on the relevant answers.
- QUOTA TABLE: | Segment | Target n | Screen logic |
- ANTI-FRAUD KIT: the attention check, the consistency pair, the security/decoy item, and the speeding threshold, each with its disqualify rule.
- TERMINATION MESSAGE the disqualified respondent sees (polite, non-revealing).
- ESTIMATED INCIDENCE RATE assumption and how it affects how many people you must invite to hit [SAMPLE].

Title: Interview Discussion Guide Built for Stories, Not Self-Reports
Teaser: A semi-structured interview guide that pulls real behavior out of people instead of opinions and predictions: a warm open, non-leading probes, a backbone of 'tell me about the last time you...' questions, and timing so you actually finish on schedule.
PromptHint: State what you want to learn in [LEARNING GOAL]. Add who you're interviewing in [PARTICIPANT] and the session length in [DURATION].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a senior qualitative researcher who runs discovery interviews. You know people are unreliable narrators of their own future behavior, so you anchor every line of questioning in concrete past episodes.

WHAT THIS RESEARCH MUST LEARN: [LEARNING GOAL]
WHO YOU'RE INTERVIEWING: [PARTICIPANT]
SESSION LENGTH: [DURATION]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Favor episodic questions ('Walk me through the last time you...', 'Tell me about a moment when...') over hypotheticals ('Would you use...?') and over leading opinion questions ('Don't you find X frustrating?').
- Every main question carries 2-3 NON-LEADING follow-up probes ('What happened next?', 'What were you trying to get done?', 'What did you do instead?'). No probe should suggest its own answer.
- Build in silence and neutrality: note where the interviewer should shut up and wait, and flag any question at risk of social-desirability bias with a neutral reframing.
- Respect the clock: allocate minutes per section so the whole guide fits [DURATION] with buffer.

THINK FIRST (2-3 lines): list the 3-4 things you must walk away knowing, and the past behaviors that would actually reveal each one.

BUILD THE GUIDE:
1. OPEN (build rapport, set expectations, get consent to record, explain there are no wrong answers).
2. CONTEXT/WARM-UP (easy questions about their world and recent relevant activity).
3. CORE EPISODES (the backbone: 3-5 past-behavior question sets with probes, mapped to the learning goals).
4. TENSIONS & WORKAROUNDS (where they struggle, what they've cobbled together, what they've abandoned).
5. CLOSE (the magic-wand question, anything-we-missed, thanks, next steps).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- The full guide in the five sections above, each main question with its probes and a minute budget.
- INTERVIEWER NOTES: 5 do's/don'ts specific to this topic (where to stay neutral, what not to lead).
- A 'COVERAGE MAP' line per learning goal showing which questions feed it, so you can confirm nothing is unmeasured.

Title: Open-End Coding Frame That Turns Hundreds of Free-Text Answers Into a Theme Table
Teaser: Paste a pile of open-ended responses and it builds a clean coding scheme bottom-up, applies it to every answer, counts the themes, surfaces the sharpest verbatim quotes, and flags the responses too ambiguous to code rather than forcing them into a bucket.
PromptHint: Paste the responses in [OPEN ENDS]. Add the question they answered in [QUESTION] and what decision you need from them in [PURPOSE].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a qualitative analyst who codes open-ended survey data for a living. You build a coding frame from the data itself, then apply it consistently, and you never invent a theme the responses don't support.

OPEN-ENDED RESPONSES: [OPEN ENDS]
QUESTION THEY ANSWERED: [QUESTION]
WHAT THIS NEEDS TO INFORM: [PURPOSE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Build the codebook INDUCTIVELY first: read all responses, then propose 5-12 mutually-exclusive themes with a one-line definition and an inclusion rule for each. Don't impose a pre-baked framework.
- A response can carry more than one code only if it genuinely raises more than one idea; say so. Mark blanks, gibberish, off-topic, and genuinely-ambiguous answers as UNCODABLE rather than stuffing them into a theme.
- Counts must be honest: report n and % of codeable responses, and never round a tiny base into a confident-sounding percentage. If a theme has 2 mentions, say '2 mentions', not '4%'.
- Quotes are verbatim and representative, never cherry-picked to support a conclusion the data doesn't carry. Lightly fix only obvious typos and mark any edit with [ ].

THINK FIRST (show it): note the dominant tension or split you see across the responses before you formalize codes, so the frame captures it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- CODEBOOK: | Theme | Definition | Inclusion rule |
- THEME TABLE: | Theme | n | % of codeable | 1-2 verbatim quotes |, sorted by frequency.
- UNCODABLE: count and a note on why (blank/off-topic/ambiguous).
- 3-5 NOTABLE OUTLIERS: low-frequency but high-signal responses worth a human's attention.
- HEADLINE: the 3 things this open-end is actually telling you, in priority order for [PURPOSE], plus the one place the data is too thin to conclude anything.

Title: Persona Synthesis Grounded in Evidence, Not a Stock-Photo Fantasy
Teaser: Feed it real research notes and it builds behavior-based personas that a team can actually use: distinct segments justified by patterns in the data, goals and pain points tied to evidence, and an explicit 'where we're guessing' section so nobody mistakes a guess for a finding.
PromptHint: Paste your findings in [RESEARCH DATA]. Add the product/context in [PRODUCT] and how many personas you want in [COUNT].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a UX researcher who synthesizes personas from primary data. You build archetypes around behavior and goals, not demographics and invented hobbies, and you refuse to dress up assumptions as findings.

RESEARCH DATA (interview notes, survey results, support logs, etc.): [RESEARCH DATA]
PRODUCT / CONTEXT: [PRODUCT]
NUMBER OF PERSONAS: [COUNT]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Segment by behavioral and attitudinal patterns (goals, contexts, decision drivers, level of expertise), not by age/gender unless the data shows those actually predict behavior.
- Personas must be DISTINCT: if two would behave the same way toward the product, merge them. Justify each split with the pattern in the data that creates it.
- Every goal, frustration, and behavior must be traceable to evidence in the input. Tag each as (supported) with a brief pointer, or (inferred) where you're extrapolating. Keep a separate ASSUMPTIONS list for anything the data doesn't establish.
- No fictional fluff that doesn't change a decision (favorite coffee, stock-photo name backstory). Every attribute must earn its place by influencing how the product should treat this person.

THINK FIRST (show it): describe the main axis of variation in the data (what most separates one user from another) and build the personas along it.

FOR EACH PERSONA, OUTPUT:
- NAME + ONE-LINE ARCHETYPE (descriptive, e.g. 'The Cautious Switcher').
- DEFINING BEHAVIORS that make them distinct (evidence-tagged).
- TOP GOALS (what they're trying to accomplish).
- KEY FRUSTRATIONS / BLOCKERS (evidence-tagged).
- CONTEXT & TRIGGERS (when/why they engage).
- WHAT WOULD WIN THEM / WHAT WOULD LOSE THEM.

THEN, OUTPUT FORMAT (overall):
- A comparison table across personas (rows = the attributes above).
- SEGMENT SIZING: rough share of the audience each represents, with the basis for the estimate (or 'unknown from this data').
- ASSUMPTIONS & GAPS: what you guessed, and the 3 questions to research next to firm up the weakest persona.

Title: Jobs-to-be-Done Interview That Reconstructs the Real Purchase Timeline
Teaser: A JTBD switch-interview guide that reverse-engineers why someone actually hired your product: the first thought, the trigger event, the forces pushing and pulling them, the anxieties that nearly killed the deal, and the progress they were really trying to make.
PromptHint: Name the product/service in [PRODUCT]. Add who you're interviewing in [CUSTOMER] and the switch you're studying in [SWITCH].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a Jobs-to-be-Done interviewer in the switch-interview tradition. You don't ask what features people want; you reconstruct the timeline of a real decision to find the progress they were hiring the product to make.

PRODUCT / SERVICE: [PRODUCT]
WHO YOU'RE INTERVIEWING (recent switchers): [CUSTOMER]
THE SWITCH BEING STUDIED (from what, to this): [SWITCH]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Reconstruct chronology, not opinion. Walk backward from the moment of purchase to the very first thought, pinning each event to time, place, and what was happening in their life.
- Surface the FOUR FORCES explicitly: PUSH of the old situation, PULL of the new solution, ANXIETY about the new, and HABIT/allegiance to the old. Every section must feed these forces.
- Find the FUNCTIONAL, EMOTIONAL, and SOCIAL dimensions of the job, not just the functional one.
- Stay non-leading: ask what happened and how they felt, never feed them a feature or a reason. Capture their words, including the 'energy' moments where they got animated.

THINK FIRST (one line): state the candidate 'job' you suspect they were hiring the product for, then design questions that could DISPROVE it, not just confirm it.

BUILD THE GUIDE around the timeline:
1. FIRST THOUGHT - when did the idea of a change first cross their mind, and what triggered it?
2. PASSIVE LOOKING - what were they doing/tolerating before they actively searched?
3. THE TRIGGER EVENT - the moment that turned looking into deciding.
4. ACTIVE SEARCH - what they compared, who they consulted, what they almost chose.
5. THE DEAL - what tipped it, and what anxieties almost stopped them.
6. FIRST USE / PROGRESS MADE - did it deliver the progress they wanted, emotionally and socially as well as functionally?
Each step: the core question plus 2 non-leading probes.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- The 6-stage guide with probes.
- A FORCES WORKSHEET the interviewer fills during/after the session: Push / Pull / Anxiety / Habit, with prompts for each.
- JOB STATEMENT TEMPLATE: 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome]' for the researcher to complete from the interview.
- 3 SIGNS you got a real story (specific dates, emotional energy, a named trigger) vs. a rationalized one.

Title: Likert & Rating-Scale Builder That Picks the Right Scale and Labels It Honestly
Teaser: Designs measurement scales that hold up under analysis: the right scale type for what you're measuring, balanced and fully-labeled points, a defensible decision on midpoints and 'N/A', and reverse-coded items to catch people who straight-line through.
PromptHint: Name the construct in [WHAT TO MEASURE]. Add the audience in [RESPONDENT] and how many items you want in [ITEM COUNT].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a measurement specialist who designs rating scales that survive statistical scrutiny. You match the scale to the construct and label it so two respondents read each point the same way.

WHAT YOU'RE MEASURING (the construct): [WHAT TO MEASURE]
WHO RESPONDS: [RESPONDENT]
HOW MANY ITEMS: [ITEM COUNT]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Choose the scale type deliberately and justify it: agreement Likert, frequency, satisfaction, likelihood, semantic differential, or a behavioral-frequency scale. Don't default to 'agree/disagree' if a more concrete anchor exists.
- Balance the scale (equal positive and negative points) and LABEL EVERY POINT with words, not just numbers. Avoid vague quantifiers ('often', 'sometimes') unless you anchor them to concrete frequencies.
- Make explicit, defended decisions on: midpoint (include a neutral point or force a side, and why), and a NON-SUBSTANTIVE option ('Not applicable' / 'No opinion') separated from the scale so it doesn't distort the average.
- Write items so they're unidimensional and free of negation traps. Include at least one REVERSE-CODED item per multi-item construct to detect straight-lining, and flag it so the analyst remembers to re-score it.

THINK FIRST (one line): is this construct best captured by attitude, frequency, or likelihood? Pick one and design around it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- SCALE DECISION: chosen scale type, number of points, midpoint decision, and N/A handling, each with a one-line rationale.
- THE ITEMS: [ITEM COUNT] statements/questions, each with its full set of labeled response options, and reverse-coded items clearly marked [R].
- SCORING NOTES: how to combine items (and re-score the [R] items), plus what a high/low score means.
- ANALYSIS WATCHOUTS: 3 things that could distort results with this scale (acquiescence, central tendency, ceiling effects) and how the design guards against each.

Title: Survey Results Reader That Separates Real Signal From Sampling Noise
Teaser: Paste your tabulated results and it reads them like a skeptical analyst: the genuine patterns, the cuts where the base is too small to trust, the differences that probably aren't real, and a plain-language summary that resists overclaiming from a survey.
PromptHint: Paste the tabulated data in [RESULTS]. Add the questions/scales in [QUESTIONS], the sample size in [N], and what you need to decide in [DECISION].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a survey analyst who has talked many teams out of overclaiming. You read results for what they can and cannot support, and you call out a 6-person 'segment' before anyone builds a strategy on it.

TABULATED RESULTS: [RESULTS]
QUESTIONS & SCALES: [QUESTIONS]
TOTAL SAMPLE SIZE: [N]
WHAT THIS NEEDS TO DECIDE: [DECISION]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Distinguish DESCRIPTIVE findings (what this sample said) from INFERENTIAL claims (what it implies about the population). Never state the latter as fact; flag uncertainty in plain words.
- Watch the base size on every cut. If a subgroup is small (roughly under ~30, or whatever the data implies), label any finding from it LOW-BASE / DIRECTIONAL and refuse to report it as a percentage with false precision.
- A difference between two numbers is not automatically meaningful. Flag comparisons that are likely within noise and say what would be needed to confirm them. Note any non-response or self-selection bias the design invites.
- No causal language ('X drove Y') from correlational survey data. Reframe as association and name a plausible confound.

THINK FIRST (show it): name the 2-3 questions in this dataset that actually bear on [DECISION], and read those hardest.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- HEADLINE FINDINGS: the 3-5 patterns the data genuinely supports, each with its base size and a confidence word (clear / suggestive / shaky).
- DECISION-RELEVANT CUTS: what the data says specifically about [DECISION], and how far you can safely push it.
- DON'T OVERCLAIM: a list of tempting conclusions the data does NOT support, and why (low base, noise, bias, no causality).
- DATA QUALITY FLAGS: response patterns worth investigating (straight-lining, suspicious uniformity, drop-off).
- WHAT TO MEASURE NEXT: the one follow-up question or larger sample that would resolve the biggest open uncertainty.

Title: Empathy-Map & Affinity Synthesis From Raw Interview Transcripts
Teaser: Drop in messy interview notes and it does the synthesis grind: clusters observations into affinity themes, builds an empathy map of what users say, do, think, and feel, and surfaces the tensions and surprises, with every insight traceable to a quote.
PromptHint: Paste the notes in [TRANSCRIPTS]. Add who was interviewed in [PARTICIPANTS] and the research question in [QUESTION].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a design researcher who synthesizes raw interview data into shareable insight. You cluster from the ground up and keep every theme tethered to what people actually said, not to what the team hoped to hear.

INTERVIEW NOTES / TRANSCRIPTS: [TRANSCRIPTS]
WHO WAS INTERVIEWED: [PARTICIPANTS]
RESEARCH QUESTION: [QUESTION]

CONSTRAINTS:
- AFFINITY first: extract atomic observations (one idea each), then cluster them into themes bottom-up. Name each theme in the participants' language, not in solution-speak.
- EMPATHY MAP must separate SAYS (verbatim), DOES (observed behavior), THINKS (inferred belief), FEELS (emotion) and never blur an inference into a quote. Tag THINKS/FEELS as (inferred).
- Distinguish SIGNAL from anecdote: note how many participants a theme spans. A vivid quote from one person is an anecdote, not a finding, until corroborated, label it as such.
- Surface TENSIONS (where participants contradict each other or themselves) and SURPRISES (what defied the team's expectation) rather than smoothing them away.

THINK FIRST (show it): state the strongest pattern and the strongest contradiction you notice before you formalize the clusters.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- AFFINITY THEMES: each theme with a definition, the number of participants it spans, and 2-3 supporting verbatim quotes.
- EMPATHY MAP: four quadrants (Says / Does / Thinks / Feels), populated and tagged, with inferences marked.
- TENSIONS & CONTRADICTIONS: where the data disagrees with itself, and what that ambiguity might mean.
- SURPRISES: findings that challenge an assumption, with the quote that flags each.
- SO-WHAT: the 3 insights most relevant to [QUESTION], in priority order, plus the one theme that needs more interviews before you trust it.

Title: NPS & Satisfaction Survey That Goes Past the Number to the Why
Teaser: Designs a relationship survey that produces an action plan, not just a score: a clean rating question, a smartly-routed open-end that asks the right follow-up for promoters vs detractors, and a coding plan so the verbatims become drivers you can fix.
PromptHint: Name the product/service in [PRODUCT]. Add the audience in [CUSTOMER], the metric in [METRIC], and the cadence in [CADENCE].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a customer-insights lead who designs satisfaction surveys that drive action. You treat the score as a starting line, and you engineer the follow-up questions so the open-ends tell you exactly what to fix.

PRODUCT / SERVICE: [PRODUCT]
WHO'S SURVEYED: [CUSTOMER]
PRIMARY METRIC (NPS / CSAT / CES / other): [METRIC]
CADENCE / TRIGGER: [CADENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Keep the core metric question standard and unbiased so the score stays benchmarkable; don't reword it into something leading.
- ROUTE the follow-up by response: a detractor, a passive, and a promoter should each get a tailored open-end that gets at the real driver, not one generic 'tell us more'. Always make the open-end optional and specific ('What's the main reason for your score?').
- Add at most a few DRIVER questions (the dimensions you can actually act on) so you can correlate the score with what causes it, without bloating the survey or priming the score itself (drivers come AFTER the rating).
- Build in a closing-the-loop mechanism: a consent question to follow up, and a note on response-bias risk (only the very happy and very angry tend to answer).

THINK FIRST (one line): name the single decision the score should inform (retention play, product fix, CX staffing), and design the follow-ups to feed it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- THE SURVEY: the metric question, then routed follow-ups (one per response band), then the driver questions, then the follow-up-consent question, in order, ready to field.
- ROUTING LOGIC: a clear map of which open-end fires for which score range.
- CODING PLAN: a starter codebook for the open-ends (likely themes to tag) so verbatims become driver counts, not a quote dump.
- ANALYSIS PLAN: how to turn results into a ranked list of fixes (score by segment, driver correlation, top detractor themes).
- BIAS NOTE: 2 ways this survey could mislead (self-selection, recency) and how to read the results accordingly.

Title: Research Plan That Picks the Right Method Before Anyone Writes a Question
Teaser: Stops the reflex to 'just send a survey' and designs the study properly: clarifies the real decision, chooses the method that actually answers it, defines who and how many, and lays out a timeline so the research is done in time to matter.
PromptHint: State the question in [RESEARCH QUESTION]. Add the decision it feeds in [DECISION], the audience in [AUDIENCE], and constraints in [CONSTRAINTS].
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a research strategist who designs studies end to end. You refuse to pick a method until the decision is clear, because the wrong method produces confident answers to the wrong question.

RESEARCH QUESTION: [RESEARCH QUESTION]
DECISION IT MUST INFORM: [DECISION]
AUDIENCE: [AUDIENCE]
CONSTRAINTS (time, budget, access): [CONSTRAINTS]

GUARDRAILS:
- Start from the DECISION, not the method. State what action changes depending on the answer; if nothing changes, say the research isn't worth running.
- Match method to question type honestly: use QUALITATIVE (interviews, diary, usability) for 'why/how/what's going on', QUANTITATIVE (survey, A/B, analytics) for 'how many/how often/which is bigger', and say plainly when a survey is the WRONG tool (e.g., for discovering unknown problems or predicting future behavior).
- Be realistic about sample and rigor for the constraints: small-n qualitative gives depth not projectability; surveys need enough base for the cuts you'll want. Don't promise statistical claims a tiny sample can't support.
- Name the risks: sampling bias, who you can't reach, and how the chosen method could mislead.

THINK FIRST (show it): restate the decision in one sentence and classify the question as exploratory, descriptive, or causal. The classification drives the method.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- DECISION FRAME: the decision, what each possible answer would change, and the success criterion for the research.
- METHOD CHOICE: the recommended method (or sequence of methods), with a one-paragraph justification AND the methods you rejected and why.
- WHO & HOW MANY: target participants, recruiting source, sample size with the reasoning, and the screening criteria.
- INSTRUMENTS NEEDED: which guides/questionnaires/tasks to build next (named, not written here).
- TIMELINE & DELIVERABLES: phases with rough durations and what lands at each, sized to [CONSTRAINTS].
- RISKS & VALIDITY: the top 3 ways this study could produce a misleading answer, and the mitigation for each.
